title: Sudden Blindness in One Eye in Dogs: Causes and Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Sudden vision loss in one eye is a veterinary emergency. Learn about causes, why immediate care is critical, and how a photo helps triage.
content_html:
What is Sudden Single-Eye Blindness?

Sudden blindness in one eye, or unilateral vision loss, occurs when a dog rapidly loses the ability to see through one eye. This can be caused by various underlying issues, including sudden pressure changes, retinal detachment, or inflammation. You may notice your dog bumping into furniture on one side, having a dilated pupil that does not shrink in light, or showing visible cloudiness in the eye.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, sudden blindness in one eye is a critical medical emergency. Many causes, such as acute glaucoma or anterior uveitis, are extremely painful and can lead to permanent, irreversible blindness within just a few hours. Seeking immediate veterinary care is the only way to manage pain and potentially save your dog's sight.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

When you call an emergency vet, being able to provide a clear photo of your dog's eyes can be vital for triage. A photo helps the medical team see the size of the pupils, the color of the eye, and any discharge or cloudiness before your dog arrives. This information allows the staff to prepare for your arrival and prioritize your pet's care based on the visible severity of the symptoms.

faqs.question: What are common causes of sudden blindness in one eye?
faqs.answer: Common causes include acute glaucoma (high eye pressure), retinal detachment, sudden acquired retinal degeneration syndrome (SARDS), or severe internal inflammation known as uveitis.

faqs.question: Can my dog still lead a normal life if the blindness is permanent?
faqs.answer: Yes, most dogs adapt very well to vision loss in one eye, provided the underlying cause is managed and any associated pain is controlled by a veterinarian.

faqs.question: Will my dog show signs of pain with sudden blindness?
faqs.answer: Often, yes. Signs of eye pain include squinting, pawing at the face, excessive tearing, or becoming unusually lethargic or withdrawn.
